## deepwiki
DeepWiki MCP provides AI-powered documentation for GitHub repositories.

Available tools:
- read_wiki_structure: Get a list of documentation topics for a repository
- read_wiki_contents: View full documentation about a repository
- ask_question: Ask any question about a repository and get an AI-powered answer
- list_available_repos: List your available repositories (private mode only)
- generate_wiki: Generate a codebase wiki for a repository — only use when explicitly requested by the user (private mode only)
- devin_knowledge_manage: Manage Devin knowledge notes and suggestions — list, search, get, create, update, delete notes, view folder structure, list/view/dismiss knowledge suggestions (private mode only)
- devin_playbook_manage: Manage Devin playbooks — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)

- devin_session_search: Search and filter Devin sessions (private mode only)

## pencil
The Pencil MCP server is the editor for reading and writing to .pen files and gives you tools to be an expert design generator and validator specifically for web and mobile applications and websites.

- IMPORTANT: The contents of the .pen files are encrypted and can be only access via the "pencil" MCP tools. DO NOT use "Read" or "Grep" tools to read contents of .pen files.
- IMPORTANT: Make sure to ONLY use the "pencil" MCP tools when reading or searching contents of .pen files (batch_get) and making changes inside .pen files (batch_design).
- IMPORTANT: Always follow the tool definitions and formats for the "pencil" MCP tools.

Available tools:
- "get_editor_state({ include_schema })": Start with this tool if you are unaware of the current editor state. Use this tool to determine in the beginning of a task, to understand what is the currently active .pen file you want to work on and the current user selection in it and other essential context information.
- "open_document(filePathOrNew)": When there is no active editor opened, pass 'new' to create an empty .pen file or if the user explicitly ask to open a specific .pen file use its path to open it.
- "get_guidelines(category?, name?, params?)": Load guides and styles for working with .pen files.
- "batch_get(patterns, nodeIds)": Retrieve nodes by searching for matching patterns or by reading node ids in batches. Use this for discovering and understand .pen files.
- "batch_design(operations)": Use it when designing with .pen files to execute multiple insert/copy/update/replace/move/delete/image operations in a single call. IMPORTANT: MAKE SURE TO FOLLOW THE OPERATION SYNTAX DEFINED IN THE TOOL'S DESCRIPTION. Aim for maximum 25 operations per call—enough to make meaningful progress without overwhelming the system. Available operations (used as a small script where every line a single operation call):
	- Insert: foo=I("parent", { ... })
	- Copy: baz=C("nodeid", "parent", { ...})
	- Replace: foo2=R("nodeid1/nodeid2", {...})
	- Update: U(foo+"/nodeid", {...})
	- Dele… [truncated]

## plugin:qmd-plugin:qmd
QMD is your local search engine over 34 markdown documents.

Collections (scope with `collection` parameter):
  - "electrobun-python-sidecar-extraction" (8 docs)
  - "my-electrobun-extraction" (5 docs)
  - "my-electrobun-docs" (11 docs)
  - "my-electrobun-temporal" (4 docs)
  - "electrobun-python-sidecar-temporal" (4 docs)
  - "electrobun-python-sidecar-brief" (1 docs)
  - "my-electrobun-brief" (1 docs)

Search: Use `query` with sub-queries (lex/vec/hyde):
  - type:'lex' — BM25 keyword search (exact terms, fast)
  - type:'vec' — semantic vector search (meaning-based)
  - type:'hyde' — hypothetical document (write what the answer looks like)

  Always provide `intent` on every search call to disambiguate and improve snippets.

Examples:
  Quick keyword lookup: [{type:'lex', query:'error handling'}]
  Semantic search: [{type:'vec', query:'how to handle errors gracefully'}]
  Best results: [{type:'lex', query:'error'}, {type:'vec', query:'error handling best practices'}]
  With intent: searches=[{type:'lex', query:'performance'}], intent='web page load times'

Retrieval:
  - `get` — single document by path or docid (#abc123). Supports line offset (`file.md:100`).
  - `multi_get` — batch retrieve by glob (`journals/2025-05*.md`) or comma-separated list.

Tips:
  - File paths in results are relative to their collection.
  - Use `minScore: 0.5` to filter low-confidence results.
  - Results include a `context` field describing the content type.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React
- ccc: This skill should be used when code search is needed (whether explicitly requested or as part of completing a task), when indexing the codebase after changes, or when the user asks about ccc, cocoindex-code, or the codebase index. Trigger phrases include 'search the codebase', 'find code related to', 'update the index', 'ccc', 'cocoindex-code'.
- vercel-react-best-practices: React and Next.js performance optimization guidelines from Vercel Engineering. This skill should be used when writing, reviewing, or refactoring React/Next.js code to ensure optimal performance patterns. Triggers on tasks involving React components, Next.js pages, data fetching, bundle optimization, or performance improvements.
- ast-grep: Guide for writing ast-grep rules to perform structural code search and analysis. Use when users need to search codebases using Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) patterns, find specific code structures, or perform complex code queries that go beyond simple text search. This skill should be used when users ask to search for code patterns, find specific language constructs, or locate code with particular structural characteristics.
- firecrawl: Official Firecrawl CLI skill for web scraping, search, crawling, and browser automation. Returns clean LLM-optimized markdown.

USE FOR:
- Web search and research
- Scraping pages, docs, and articles
- Site mapping and bulk content extraction
- Browser automation for interactive pages

Must be pre-installed and authenticated. See rules/install.md for setup, rules/security.md for output handling.

- bmad-cis-storytelling: Craft compelling narratives using story frameworks. Use when the user says "help me with storytelling" or "I want to create a narrative through storytelling"
- bmad-sprint-status: Summarize sprint status and surface risks. Use when the user says "check sprint status" or "show sprint status"
- bmad-agent-tech-writer: Technical documentation specialist and knowledge curator. Use when the user asks to talk to Paige or requests the tech writer.
- bmad-review-adversarial-general: Perform a Cynical Review and produce a findings report. Use when the user requests a critical review of something
- bmad-module-builder: Plans, creates, and validates BMad modules. Use when the user requests to 'ideate module', 'plan a module', 'create module', 'build a module', or 'validate module'.
- feature-dev:feature-dev: Guided feature development with codebase understanding and architecture focus
- qmd-plugin:qmd: Search personal markdown knowledge bases, notes, meeting transcripts, and documentation using QMD - a local hybrid search engine. Combines BM25 keyword search, vector semantic search, and LLM re-ranking. Use when users ask to search notes, find documents, look up information in their knowledge base, retrieve meeting notes, or search documentation. Triggers on "search markdown files", "search my notes", "find in docs", "look up", "what did I write about", "meeting notes about".
- claude-mem:do: Execute a phased implementation plan using subagents. Use when asked to execute, run, or carry out a plan — especially one created by make-plan.
- claude-mem:make-plan: Create a detailed, phased implementation plan with documentation discovery. Use when asked to plan a feature, task, or multi-step implementation — especially before executing with do.
- claude-mem:mem-search: Search claude-mem's persistent cross-session memory database. Use when user asks "did we already solve this?", "how did we do X last time?", or needs work from previous sessions.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

file.content:
---
main_config: '{project-root}/_bmad/bmm/config.yaml'
---

# Code Review Workflow

**Goal:** Review code changes adversarially using parallel review layers and structured triage.

**Your Role:** You are an elite code reviewer. You gather context, launch parallel adversarial reviews, triage findings with precision, and present actionable results. No noise, no filler.


## WORKFLOW ARCHITECTURE

This uses **step-file architecture** for disciplined execution:

- **Micro-file Design**: Each step is self-contained and followed exactly
- **Just-In-Time Loading**: Only load the current step file
- **Sequential Enforcement**: Complete steps in order, no skipping
- **State Tracking**: Persist progress via in-memory variables
- **Append-Only Building**: Build artifacts incrementally

### Step Processing Rules

1. **READ COMPLETELY**: Read the entire step file before acting
2. **FOLLOW SEQUENCE**: Execute sections in order
3. **WAIT FOR INPUT**: Halt at checkpoints and wait for human
4. **LOAD NEXT**: When directed, read fully and follow the next step file

### Critical Rules (NO EXCEPTIONS)

- **NEVER** load multiple step files simultaneously
- **ALWAYS** read entire step file before execution
- **NEVER** skip steps or optimize the sequence
- **ALWAYS** follow the exact instructions in the step file
- **ALWAYS** halt at checkpoints and wait for human input


## INITIALIZATION SEQUENCE

### 1. Configuration Loading

Load and read full config from `{main_config}` and resolve:

- `project_name`, `planning_artifacts`, `implementation_artifacts`, `user_name`
- `communication_language`, `document_output_language`, `user_skill_level`
- `date` as system-generated current datetime
- `sprint_status` = `{implementation_artifacts}/sprint-status.yaml`
- `project_context` = `**/project-context.md` (load if exists)
- CLAUDE.md / memory files (load if exist)

YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`.

### 2. First Step Execution

Read fully and follow: `./steps/step-01-gather-context.md` to begin the workflow.

1	---
2	diff_output: '' # set at runtime
3	spec_file: '' # set at runtime (path or empty)
4	review_mode: '' # set at runtime: "full" or "no-spec"
5	story_key: '' # set at runtime when discovered from sprint status
6	---
7	
8	# Step 1: Gather Context
9	
10	## RULES
11	
12	- YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
13	- The prompt that triggered this workflow IS the intent — not a hint.
14	- Do not modify any files. This step is read-only.
15	
16	## INSTRUCTIONS
17	
18	1. **Find the review target.** The conversation context before this skill was triggered IS your starting point — not a blank slate. Check in this order — stop as soon as the review target is identified:
19	
20	   **Tier 1 — Explicit argument.**
21	   Did the user pass a PR, commit SHA, branch, spec file, or diff source this message?
22	   - PR reference → resolve to branch/commit via `gh pr view`. If resolution fails, ask for a SHA or branch.
23	   - Commit or branch → use directly.
24	   - Spec file → set `{spec_file}` to the provided path. Check its frontmatter for `baseline_commit`. If found, use as diff baseline. If not found, continue the cascade (a spec alone does not identify a diff source).
25	   - Also scan the argument for diff-mode keywords that narrow the scope:
26	     - "staged" / "staged changes" → Staged changes only
27	     - "uncommitted" / "working tree" / "all changes" → Uncommitted changes (staged + unstaged)
28	     - "branch diff" / "vs main" / "against main" / "compared to <branch>" → Branch diff (extract base branch if mentioned)
29	     - "commit range" / "last N commits" / "<from-sha>..<to-sha>" → Specific commit range
30	     - "this diff" / "provided diff" / "paste" → User-provided diff (do not match bare "diff" — it appears in other modes)
31	   - When multiple keywords match, prefer the most specific (e.g., "branch diff" over bare "diff").
32	
33	   **Tier 2 — Recent conversation.**
34	   Do the last few messages reveal what the user wants to be reviewed? Look for spec paths, commit refs, branches, PRs, or descriptions of a change. Apply the same diff-mode keyword scan and routing as Tier 1.
35	
36	   **Tier 3 — Sprint tracking.**
37	   Look for a sprint status file (`*sprint-status*`) in `{implementation_artifacts}` or `{planning_artifacts}`. If found, scan for stories with status `review`:
38	   - **Exactly one `review` story:** Set `{story_key}` to the story's key (e.g., `1-2-user-auth`). Suggest it: "I found story <story-id> in `review` status. Would you like to review its changes? [Y] Yes / [N] No, let me choose". If confirmed, use the story context to determine the diff source (branch name derived from story slug, or uncommitted changes). If declined, clear `{story_key}` and fall through.
39	   - **Multiple `review` stories:** Present them as numbered options alongside a manual choice option. Wait for user selection. If a story is selected, set `{story_key}` and use its context to determine the diff source. If manual choice is selected, clear `{story_key}` and fall through.
40	   - **None:** Fall through.
41	
42	   **Tier 4 — Current git state.**
43	   If version control is unavailable, skip to Tier 5. Otherwise, check the current branch and HEAD. If the branch is not `main` (or the default branch), confirm: "I see HEAD is `<short-sha>` on `<branch>` — do you want to review this branch's changes?" If confirmed, treat as a branch diff against `main`. If declined, fall through.
44	
45	   **Tier 5 — Ask.**
46	   Fall through to instruction 2.
47	
48	   Never ask extra questions beyond what the cascade prescribes. If a tier above already identified the target, skip the remaining tiers and proceed to instruction 3 (construct diff).
49	
50	2. HALT. Ask the user: **What do you want to review?** Present these options:
51	   - **Uncommitted changes** (staged + unstaged)
52	   - **Staged changes only**
53	   - **Branch diff** vs a base branch (ask which base branch)
54	   - **Specific commit range** (ask for the range)
55	   - **Provided diff or file list** (user pastes or provides a path)
56	
57	3. Construct `{diff_output}` from the chosen source.
58	   - For **staged changes only**: run `git diff --cached`.
59	   - For **uncommitted changes** (staged + unstaged): run `git diff HEAD`.
60	   - For **branch diff**: verify the base branch exists before running `git diff`. If it does not exist, HALT and ask the user for a valid branch.
61	   - For **commit range**: verify the range resolves. If it does not, HALT and ask the user for a valid range.
62	   - For **provided diff**: validate the content is non-empty and parseable as a unified diff. If it is not parseable, HALT and ask the user to provide a valid diff.
63	   - For **file list**: validate each path exists in the working tree. Construct `{diff_output}` by running `git diff HEAD -- <path1> <path2> ...`. If any paths are untracked (new files not yet staged), use `git diff --no-index /dev/null <path>` to include them. If the diff is empty (files have no uncommitted changes and are not untracked), ask the user whether to review the full file contents or to specify a different baseline.
64	   - After constructing `{diff_output}`, verify it is non-empty regardless of source type. If empty, HALT and tell the user there is nothing to review.
65	
66	4. **Set the spec context.**
67	   - If `{spec_file}` is already set (from Tier 1 or Tier 2): verify the file exists and is readable, then set `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
68	   - Otherwise, ask the user: **Is there a spec or story file that provides context for these changes?**
69	     - If yes: set `{spec_file}` to the path provided, verify the file exists and is readable, then set `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
70	     - If no: set `{review_mode}` = `"no-spec"`.
71	
72	5. If `{review_mode}` = `"full"` and the file at `{spec_file}` has a `context` field in its frontmatter listing additional docs, load each referenced document. Warn the user about any docs that cannot be found.
73	
74	6. Sanity check: if `{diff_output}` exceeds approximately 3000 lines, warn the user and offer to chunk the review by file group.
75	   - If the user opts to chunk: agree on the first group, narrow `{diff_output}` accordingly, and list the remaining groups for the user to note for follow-up runs.
76	   - If the user declines: proceed as-is with the full diff.
77	
78	### CHECKPOINT
79	
80	Present a summary before proceeding: diff stats (files changed, lines added/removed), `{review_mode}`, and loaded spec/context docs (if any). HALT and wait for user confirmation to proceed.
81	
82	
83	## NEXT
84	
85	Read fully and follow `./step-02-review.md`
86

review_mode: '' # set at runtime: "full" or "no-spec"
story_key: '' # set at runtime when discovered from sprint status
---

# Step 1: Gather Context

## RULES

- YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
- The prompt that triggered this workflow IS the intent — not a hint.
- Do not modify any files. This step is read-only.

## INSTRUCTIONS

1. **Find the review target.** The conversation context before this skill was triggered IS your starting point — not a blank slate. Check in this order — stop as soon as the review target is identified:

   **Tier 1 — Explicit argument.**
   Did the user pass a PR, commit SHA, branch, spec file, or diff source this message?
   - PR reference → resolve to branch/commit via `gh pr view`. If resolution fails, ask for a SHA or branch.
   - Commit or branch → use directly.
   - Spec file → set `{spec_file}` to the provided path. Check its frontmatter for `baseline_commit`. If found, use as diff baseline. If not found, continue the cascade (a spec alone does not identify a diff source).
   - Also scan the argument for diff-mode keywords that narrow the scope:
     - "staged" / "staged changes" → Staged changes only
     - "uncommitted" / "working tree" / "all changes" → Uncommitted changes (staged + unstaged)
     - "branch diff" / "vs main" / "against main" / "compared to <branch>" → Branch diff (extract base branch if mentioned)
     - "commit range" / "last N commits" / "<from-sha>..<to-sha>" → Specific commit range
     - "this diff" / "provided diff" / "paste" → User-provided diff (do not match bare "diff" — it appears in other modes)
   - When multiple keywords match, prefer the most specific (e.g., "branch diff" over bare "diff").

   **Tier 2 — Recent conversation.**
   Do the last few messages reveal what the user wants to be reviewed? Look for spec paths, commit refs, branches, PRs, or descriptions of a change. Apply the same diff-mode keyword scan and routing as Tier 1.

   **Tier 3 — Sprint tracking.**
   Look for a sprint status file (`*sprint-status*`) in `{implementation_artifacts}` or `{planning_artifacts}`. If found, scan for stories with status `review`:
   - **Exactly one `review` story:** Set `{story_key}` to the story's key (e.g., `1-2-user-auth`). Suggest it: "I found story <story-id> in `review` status. Would you like to review its changes? [Y] Yes / [N] No, let me choose". If confirmed, use the story context to determine the diff source (branch name derived from story slug, or uncommitted changes). If declined, clear `{story_key}` and fall through.
   - **Multiple `review` stories:** Present them as numbered options alongside a manual choice option. Wait for user selection. If a story is selected, set `{story_key}` and use its context to determine the diff source. If manual choice is selected, clear `{story_key}` and fall through.
   - **None:** Fall through.

   **Tier 4 — Current git state.**
   If version control is unavailable, skip to Tier 5. Otherwise, check the current branch and HEAD. If the branch is not `main` (or the default branch), confirm: "I see HEAD is `<short-sha>` on `<branch>` — do you want to review this branch's changes?" If confirmed, treat as a branch diff against `main`. If declined, fall through.

   **Tier 5 — Ask.**
   Fall through to instruction 2.

   Never ask extra questions beyond what the cascade prescribes. If a tier above already identified the target, skip the remaining tiers and proceed to instruction 3 (construct diff).

2. HALT. Ask the user: **What do you want to review?** Present these options:
   - **Uncommitted changes** (staged + unstaged)
   - **Staged changes only**
   - **Branch diff** vs a base branch (ask which base branch)
   - **Specific commit range** (ask for the range)
   - **Provided diff or file list** (user pastes or provides a path)

3. Construct `{diff_output}` from the chosen source.
   - For **staged changes only**: run `git diff --cached`.
   - For **uncommitted changes** (staged + unstaged): run `git diff HEAD`.
   - For **branch diff**: verify the base branch exists before running `git diff`. If it does not exist, HALT and ask the user for a valid branch.
   - For **commit range**: verify the range resolves. If it does not, HALT and ask the user for a valid range.
   - For **provided diff**: validate the content is non-empty and parseable as a unified diff. If it is not parseable, HALT and ask the user to provide a valid diff.
   - For **file list**: validate each path exists in the working tree. Construct `{diff_output}` by running `git diff HEAD -- <path1> <path2> ...`. If any paths are untracked (new files not yet staged), use `git diff --no-index /dev/null <path>` to include them. If the diff is empty (files have no uncommitted changes and are not untracked), ask the user whether to review the full file contents or to specify a different baseline.
   - After constructing `{diff_output}`, verify it is non-empty regardless of source type. If empty, HALT and tell the user there is nothing to review.

4. **Set the spec context.**
   - If `{spec_file}` is already set (from Tier 1 or Tier 2): verify the file exists and is readable, then set `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
   - Otherwise, ask the user: **Is there a spec or story file that provides context for these changes?**
     - If yes: set `{spec_file}` to the path provided, verify the file exists and is readable, then set `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
     - If no: set `{review_mode}` = `"no-spec"`.

5. If `{review_mode}` = `"full"` and the file at `{spec_file}` has a `context` field in its frontmatter listing additional docs, load each referenced document. Warn the user about any docs that cannot be found.

6. Sanity check: if `{diff_output}` exceeds approximately 3000 lines, warn the user and offer to chunk the review by file group.
   - If the user opts to chunk: agree on the first group, narrow `{diff_output}` accordingly, and list the remaining groups for the user to note for follow-up runs.
   - If the user declines: proceed as-is with the full diff.

### CHECKPOINT

Present a summary before proceeding: diff stats (files changed, lines added/removed), `{review_mode}`, and loaded spec/context docs (if any). HALT and wait for user confirmation to proceed.


## NEXT

Read fully and follow `./step-02-review.md`

# STATUS DEFINITIONS:
# ==================
# Epic Status:
#   - backlog: Epic not yet started
#   - in-progress: Epic actively being worked on
#   - done: All stories in epic completed
#
# Epic Status Transitions:
#   - backlog → in-progress: Automatically when first story is created (via create-story)
#   - in-progress → done: Manually when all stories reach 'done' status
#
# Story Status:
#   - backlog: Story only exists in epic file
#   - ready-for-dev: Story file created in stories folder
#   - in-progress: Developer actively working on implementation
#   - review: Ready for code review (via Dev's code-review workflow)
#   - done: Story completed
#
# Retrospective Status:
#   - optional: Can be completed but not required
#   - done: Retrospective has been completed
#
# WORKFLOW NOTES:
# ===============
# - Epic transitions to 'in-progress' automatically when first story is created
# - Stories can be worked in parallel if team capacity allows
# - Developer typically creates next story after previous one is 'done' to incorporate learnings
# - Dev moves story to 'review', then runs code-review (fresh context, different LLM recommended)

1	# Story 3.2: Validate `release.yaml` via alpha test cut from feature branch
2	
3	Status: review
4	
5	<!-- Note: Validation is optional. Run validate-create-story for quality check before dev-story. -->
6	
7	## Story
8	
9	As the maintainer,
10	I want the `release.yaml` workflow (authored in Story 3.1, `feat/release-workflow` HEAD `34ad130`) exercised end-to-end on a throwaway alpha version **before it touches `main`**,
11	so that OIDC trusted publishing is proven working, the SLSA-L2 provenance attestation is verified attached on npm, the `release` environment approval gate fires, the dist-tag routing is proven `alpha → npm tag alpha` (NOT `latest`), and the CHANGELOG append + `[Unreleased]` anchor lifecycle is observed against real output — eliminating the known unknowns before Story 3.3 merges the workflow to `main` and before the v1.0.0 critical path in Epic 5 ever loads.
12	
13	## Acceptance Criteria
14	
15	1. **Precondition audit — Story 3.1 / 1.2 / 1.3 outputs verified intact before dispatch.** Reviewer SHALL confirm at execution time:
16	   - `.github/workflows/release.yaml` exists on `feat/release-workflow` (literal filename, extension `.yaml` not `.yml` — Epic 1 retro carry-forward #1, enforced character-for-character by the npm Trusted Publisher).
17	   - `feat/release-workflow` HEAD is at commit `34ad130` (Story 3.1 + 5 code-review patches) or later; no unrelated commits have landed between Story 3.1 close-out and Story 3.2 dispatch.
18	   - The `release` GitHub Environment (id `14347249917`, Story 1.2) has `armelhbobdad` as a required reviewer, `prevent_self_review: false`, and **zero** env-scoped secrets. `NPM_TOKEN` remains at repo scope, NOT env scope.
19	   - The npm Trusted Publisher (Story 1.3, registered 2026-04-20) carries the four fields verbatim: `armelhbobdad` / `bmad-module-skill-forge` / **`release.yaml`** / **`release`**. Inspection is UI-only per `docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher` (no public API).
20	   - `release.yaml` has NOT been merged to `main` yet (Story 3.3 owns that merge; Story 3.2 is the gating validator).
21	   - Current `package.json` version is `0.10.0`; `npm version prerelease --preid=alpha` SHALL therefore produce `0.10.1-alpha.0` (NOT `1.0.0-alpha.0` — the v1.0.0 prerelease cycle is Story 5.2's territory, not 3.2's).
22	
23	2. **Release environment allow-list temporarily widened to `feat/release-workflow`, captured for revoke.** Per `docs/RELEASING.md § Release Environment → Temporarily allowing a feature branch`, the dev agent SHALL:
24	   - `POST /repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies` with `{"name": "feat/release-workflow"}` and capture the returned `id` into a shell variable (`POLICY_ID`).
25	   - Confirm the POST returned a numeric `id` (not an error body) before proceeding — the command-substitution pattern in `docs/RELEASING.md` prevents the "eyeball-and-paste" footgun.
26	   - Treat the revoke (AC #8) as the final step of this story's execution, NOT a follow-up. A feature branch left on the allow-list is an unguarded hole per `docs/RELEASING.md:152-153`.
27	
28	3. **Alpha cut dispatched against `feat/release-workflow`.** The `release.yaml` workflow SHALL be triggered with `version_bump: alpha` such that it runs against `feat/release-workflow` HEAD. Two candidate dispatch paths (decide at execution time per Task 3):
29	   - **Primary: `gh workflow run release.yaml --ref feat/release-workflow -f version_bump=alpha`** — uses the REST dispatch API against a non-default ref. This may or may not work depending on current GHA behavior (see Dev Notes § "Known unknown — workflow_dispatch from a non-default branch").
30	   - **Fallback: temporary `on: push: branches: [feat/release-workflow]` trigger added to `release.yaml` as a throwaway commit** — matches the Epic 1 Story 1.2 pattern (`epic-1-retro-2026-04-20.md § Patterns across all three stories → Task spec drifted from actual API/UI`, pivot to `on: push: branches: [throwaway/env-gate-test]`). If used, the trigger SHALL be reverted in a second commit on `feat/release-workflow` BEFORE Story 3.3 opens its merge PR (see AC #8).
31	   - Whichever path runs, the commit body / Dev Agent Record entry SHALL name the mechanism used and the run URL produced. Do NOT silently switch between them mid-run.
32	
33	4. **Approval gate engages and is approved.** The workflow SHALL pause at the `environment: release` step for reviewer approval:
34	   - The Actions UI "Review deployments" prompt SHALL appear for the `release` environment.
35	   - The dev agent (or designated reviewer) SHALL approve via the UI; the approval event SHALL appear in the Actions run audit trail (NFR5 reconfirm).
36	   - The resume MUST NOT be automated — human click is the gate semantic (Story 1.2 invariant). `prevent_self_review: false` permits self-approval only as a solo-maintainer compatibility flag; the approval is recorded in the audit trail either way.
37	
38	5. **Workflow steps complete clean, end-to-end.** Post-approval, every `release.yaml` step from Story 3.1 SHALL execute without error in order:
39	   - `Checkout` · `Setup Node.js` · `Ensure npm CLI supports trusted publishing` (`npm install -g npm@latest`) · `Install uv` · `Install dependencies` (`npm ci`) · `Run tests and validation` (`npm test` — full 12 subcommands).
40	   - `Configure Git` · `Bump version` (`npm version prerelease --preid=alpha`) → `0.10.1-alpha.0`.
41	   - `Get new version and previous tag` → outputs `new_version=0.10.1-alpha.0`, `previous_tag=v0.10.0`.
42	   - `Update marketplace.json version` → `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` `"version"` becomes `0.10.1-alpha.0`.
43	   - `Update CHANGELOG.md` → `npx conventional-changelog-cli -p conventionalcommits -i CHANGELOG.md -s` runs clean; a new entry is prepended **above** the `## [Unreleased]` anchor (or the anchor is consumed — Task 5 records which).
44	   - `Assert no bogus issue refs in CHANGELOG` → `grep -iE '(closes|fixes|resolves) \[#[^0-9]'` returns empty; step exits 0. (If the step trips, AC #11's defect protocol applies — this is the entire point of the guard.)
45	   - `Pre-publish dry-run` → `npm publish --dry-run` with `NPM_TOKEN: ""` passes.
46	   - `Commit version bump` · `Generate release notes` · `Create and push tag` → tag `v0.10.1-alpha.0` is pushed to `origin`.
47	   - `Push commit to main` is SKIPPED (`github.ref != 'refs/heads/main'` on a feature-branch dispatch — the Story 3.1 code-review patch D1 exists precisely for this path; the step SHALL fire `Skip main push (non-main dispatch ref)` instead, emitting `::notice::` with the expected message).
48	   - `Publish to npm via OIDC trusted publishing` → dist-tag chain selects `--tag alpha` (`VERSION == *"alpha"*`); `NPM_TOKEN: ""` is set; publish succeeds; NO `--provenance` flag passed (automatic under OIDC).
49	   - `Create GitHub Release` → `softprops/action-gh-release@v2` with `prerelease: true` (since version contains `alpha`).
50	   - `Summary` → `$GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY` receives the expected block (header, npm URL, GitHub release URL, provenance line).
51	
52	6. **npm-side verification — published, correctly tagged, provenance present.**
53	   - `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@0.10.1-alpha.0` SHALL return the package manifest (non-404).
54	   - `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge dist-tags --json` SHALL show `"alpha": "0.10.1-alpha.0"` AND `"latest"` unchanged from its current value (the prior stable, `0.10.0`). The alpha version SHALL NOT appear under `"latest"` (FR3 invariant — the default-`latest` trap documented in research doc line 193).
55	   - **Provenance attestation evidence** — at least ONE of the following SHALL confirm a SLSA Build Level 2 provenance attestation is attached (NFR4; the Axios-March-2026 attack detection signal):
56	     - `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@0.10.1-alpha.0 --json` output contains a non-null attestation reference (exact jq path varies by npm CLI version — `.dist.attestations`, top-level `.attestations`, or a `provenance`-keyed sub-object are all valid shapes; record the exact path that resolves).
57	     - `npm audit signatures --json` run against a fresh install of `bmad-module-skill-forge@0.10.1-alpha.0` reports a verified provenance signature.
58	     - The npm package page (`https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge/v/0.10.1-alpha.0`) renders a "Provenance" badge with the expected GitHub Actions build metadata (repo, workflow, commit SHA).
59	   - At least one of the above methods SHALL be captured in Debug Log References verbatim (raw jq output, raw audit output, or screenshot URL) — prose "it worked" is insufficient.
60	   - `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@0.10.1-alpha.0 --json | jq '.repository'` SHALL show the populated repository URL (required for provenance attestation to validate per research doc line 345).

# Story 3.2: Validate `release.yaml` via alpha test cut from feature branch

Status: review

<!-- Note: Validation is optional. Run validate-create-story for quality check before dev-story. -->

## Story

As the maintainer,
I want the `release.yaml` workflow (authored in Story 3.1, `feat/release-workflow` HEAD `34ad130`) exercised end-to-end on a throwaway alpha version **before it touches `main`**,
so that OIDC trusted publishing is proven working, the SLSA-L2 provenance attestation is verified attached on npm, the `release` environment approval gate fires, the dist-tag routing is proven `alpha → npm tag alpha` (NOT `latest`), and the CHANGELOG append + `[Unreleased]` anchor lifecycle is observed against real output — eliminating the known unknowns before Story 3.3 merges the workflow to `main` and before the v1.0.0 critical path in Epic 5 ever loads.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. **Precondition audit — Story 3.1 / 1.2 / 1.3 outputs verified intact before dispatch.** Reviewer SHALL confirm at execution time:
   - `.github/workflows/release.yaml` exists on `feat/release-workflow` (literal filename, extension `.yaml` not `.yml` — Epic 1 retro carry-forward #1, enforced character-for-character by the npm Trusted Publisher).
   - `feat/release-workflow` HEAD is at commit `34ad130` (Story 3.1 + 5 code-review patches) or later; no unrelated commits have landed between Story 3.1 close-out and Story 3.2 dispatch.
   - The `release` GitHub Environment (id `14347249917`, Story 1.2) has `armelhbobdad` as a required reviewer, `prevent_self_review: false`, and **zero** env-scoped secrets. `NPM_TOKEN` remains at repo scope, NOT env scope.
   - The npm Trusted Publisher (Story 1.3, registered 2026-04-20) carries the four fields verbatim: `armelhbobdad` / `bmad-module-skill-forge` / **`release.yaml`** / **`release`**. Inspection is UI-only per `docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher` (no public API).
   - `release.yaml` has NOT been merged to `main` yet (Story 3.3 owns that merge; Story 3.2 is the gating validator).
   - Current `package.json` version is `0.10.0`; `npm version prerelease --preid=alpha` SHALL therefore produce `0.10.1-alpha.0` (NOT `1.0.0-alpha.0` — the v1.0.0 prerelease cycle is Story 5.2's territory, not 3.2's).

2. **Release environment allow-list temporarily widened to `feat/release-workflow`, captured for revoke.** Per `docs/RELEASING.md § Release Environment → Temporarily allowing a feature branch`, the dev agent SHALL:
   - `POST /repos/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/environments/release/deployment-branch-policies` with `{"name": "feat/release-workflow"}` and capture the returned `id` into a shell variable (`POLICY_ID`).
   - Confirm the POST returned a numeric `id` (not an error body) before proceeding — the command-substitution pattern in `docs/RELEASING.md` prevents the "eyeball-and-paste" footgun.
   - Treat the revoke (AC #8) as the final step of this story's execution, NOT a follow-up. A feature branch left on the allow-list is an unguarded hole per `docs/RELEASING.md:152-153`.

3. **Alpha cut dispatched against `feat/release-workflow`.** The `release.yaml` workflow SHALL be triggered with `version_bump: alpha` such that it runs against `feat/release-workflow` HEAD. Two candidate dispatch paths (decide at execution time per Task 3):
   - **Primary: `gh workflow run release.yaml --ref feat/release-workflow -f version_bump=alpha`** — uses the REST dispatch API against a non-default ref. This may or may not work depending on current GHA behavior (see Dev Notes § "Known unknown — workflow_dispatch from a non-default branch").
   - **Fallback: temporary `on: push: branches: [feat/release-workflow]` trigger added to `release.yaml` as a throwaway commit** — matches the Epic 1 Story 1.2 pattern (`epic-1-retro-2026-04-20.md § Patterns across all three stories → Task spec drifted from actual API/UI`, pivot to `on: push: branches: [throwaway/env-gate-test]`). If used, the trigger SHALL be reverted in a second commit on `feat/release-workflow` BEFORE Story 3.3 opens its merge PR (see AC #8).
   - Whichever path runs, the commit body / Dev Agent Record entry SHALL name the mechanism used and the run URL produced. Do NOT silently switch between them mid-run.

4. **Approval gate engages and is approved.** The workflow SHALL pause at the `environment: release` step for reviewer approval:
   - The Actions UI "Review deployments" prompt SHALL appear for the `release` environment.
   - The dev agent (or designated reviewer) SHALL approve via the UI; the approval event SHALL appear in the Actions run audit trail (NFR5 reconfirm).
   - The resume MUST NOT be automated — human click is the gate semantic (Story 1.2 invariant). `prevent_self_review: false` permits self-approval only as a solo-maintainer compatibility flag; the approval is recorded in the audit trail either way.

5. **Workflow steps complete clean, end-to-end.** Post-approval, every `release.yaml` step from Story 3.1 SHALL execute without error in order:
   - `Checkout` · `Setup Node.js` · `Ensure npm CLI supports trusted publishing` (`npm install -g npm@latest`) · `Install uv` · `Install dependencies` (`npm ci`) · `Run tests and validation` (`npm test` — full 12 subcommands).
   - `Configure Git` · `Bump version` (`npm version prerelease --preid=alpha`) → `0.10.1-alpha.0`.
   - `Get new version and previous tag` → outputs `new_version=0.10.1-alpha.0`, `previous_tag=v0.10.0`.
   - `Update marketplace.json version` → `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` `"version"` becomes `0.10.1-alpha.0`.
   - `Update CHANGELOG.md` → `npx conventional-changelog-cli -p conventionalcommits -i CHANGELOG.md -s` runs clean; a new entry is prepended **above** the `## [Unreleased]` anchor (or the anchor is consumed — Task 5 records which).
   - `Assert no bogus issue refs in CHANGELOG` → `grep -iE '(closes|fixes|resolves) \[#[^0-9]'` returns empty; step exits 0. (If the step trips, AC #11's defect protocol applies — this is the entire point of the guard.)
   - `Pre-publish dry-run` → `npm publish --dry-run` with `NPM_TOKEN: ""` passes.
   - `Commit version bump` · `Generate release notes` · `Create and push tag` → tag `v0.10.1-alpha.0` is pushed to `origin`.
   - `Push commit to main` is SKIPPED (`github.ref != 'refs/heads/main'` on a feature-branch dispatch — the Story 3.1 code-review patch D1 exists precisely for this path; the step SHALL fire `Skip main push (non-main dispatch ref)` instead, emitting `::notice::` with the expected message).
   - `Publish to npm via OIDC trusted publishing` → dist-tag chain selects `--tag alpha` (`VERSION == *"alpha"*`); `NPM_TOKEN: ""` is set; publish succeeds; NO `--provenance` flag passed (automatic under OIDC).
   - `Create GitHub Release` → `softprops/action-gh-release@v2` with `prerelease: true` (since version contains `alpha`).
   - `Summary` → `$GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY` receives the expected block (header, npm URL, GitHub release URL, provenance line).

6. **npm-side verification — published, correctly tagged, provenance present.**
   - `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@0.10.1-alpha.0` SHALL return the package manifest (non-404).
   - `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge dist-tags --json` SHALL show `"alpha": "0.10.1-alpha.0"` AND `"latest"` unchanged from its current value (the prior stable, `0.10.0`). The alpha version SHALL NOT appear under `"latest"` (FR3 invariant — the default-`latest` trap documented in research doc line 193).
   - **Provenance attestation evidence** — at least ONE of the following SHALL confirm a SLSA Build Level 2 provenance attestation is attached (NFR4; the Axios-March-2026 attack detection signal):
     - `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@0.10.1-alpha.0 --json` output contains a non-null attestation reference (exact jq path varies by npm CLI version — `.dist.attestations`, top-level `.attestations`, or a `provenance`-keyed sub-object are all valid shapes; record the exact path that resolves).
     - `npm audit signatures --json` run against a fresh install of `bmad-module-skill-forge@0.10.1-alpha.0` reports a verified provenance signature.
     - The npm package page (`https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge/v/0.10.1-alpha.0`) renders a "Provenance" badge with the expected GitHub Actions build metadata (repo, workflow, commit SHA).
   - At least one of the above methods SHALL be captured in Debug Log References verbatim (raw jq output, raw audit output, or screenshot URL) — prose "it worked" is insufficient.
   - `npm view bmad-module-skill-forge@0.10.1-alpha.0 --json | jq '.repository'` SHALL show the populated repository URL (required for provenance attestation to validate per research doc line 345).

The sprint-status note ("4 load-bearing defect-fixes applied") points to the release.yaml fixes as Story 3.2's real review target. The export-skill changes belong to whatever story produced them on `dev`.

Before I proceed, I want to confirm scope. Options:

**[A]** Review **only release.yaml defect-fixes** (`34ad130..HEAD -- .github/workflows/release.yaml`) — the ~26-line delta that actually matters for Story 3.2's approval.
**[B]** Review **full Story 3.2 delta** (`34ad130..HEAD`, all 8 files, ~120 lines) — includes merged-in export-skill changes as a bystander.

file.content:
---
failed_layers: '' # set at runtime: comma-separated list of layers that failed or returned empty
---

# Step 2: Review

## RULES

- YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
- The Blind Hunter subagent receives NO project context — diff only.
- The Edge Case Hunter subagent receives diff and project read access.
- The Acceptance Auditor subagent receives diff, spec, and context docs.

## INSTRUCTIONS

1. If `{review_mode}` = `"no-spec"`, note to the user: "Acceptance Auditor skipped — no spec file provided."

2. Launch parallel subagents without conversation context. If subagents are not available, generate prompt files in `{implementation_artifacts}` — one per reviewer role below — and HALT. Ask the user to run each in a separate session (ideally a different LLM) and paste back the findings. When findings are pasted, resume from this point and proceed to step 3.

   - **Blind Hunter** — receives `{diff_output}` only. No spec, no context docs, no project access. Invoke via the `bmad-review-adversarial-general` skill.

   - **Edge Case Hunter** — receives `{diff_output}` and read access to the project. Invoke via the `bmad-review-edge-case-hunter` skill.

   - **Acceptance Auditor** (only if `{review_mode}` = `"full"`) — receives `{diff_output}`, the content of the file at `{spec_file}`, and any loaded context docs. Its prompt:
     > You are an Acceptance Auditor. Review this diff against the spec and context docs. Check for: violations of acceptance criteria, deviations from spec intent, missing implementation of specified behavior, contradictions between spec constraints and actual code. Output findings as a Markdown list. Each finding: one-line title, which AC/constraint it violates, and evidence from the diff.

3. **Subagent failure handling**: If any subagent fails, times out, or returns empty results, append the layer name to `{failed_layers}` (comma-separated) and proceed with findings from the remaining layers.

4. Collect all findings from the completed layers.


## NEXT

Read fully and follow `./step-03-triage.md`

diff --git a/.github/workflows/release.yaml b/.github/workflows/release.yaml
index 4f5e6b1..9c67b9f 100644
--- a/.github/workflows/release.yaml
+++ b/.github/workflows/release.yaml
@@ -34,6 +34,11 @@ jobs:
   release:
     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
     environment: release
+    env:
+      # Husky's documented opt-out (.husky/_/h checks this explicitly). Needed
+      # because .husky/commit-msg invokes `entire` (local dev session tool) and
+      # hard-exits if it's not on PATH — breaks CI's `Commit version bump`.
+      HUSKY: "0"
     steps:
       - name: Checkout
         uses: actions/checkout@v4
@@ -49,7 +54,17 @@ jobs:
           registry-url: https://registry.npmjs.org
 
       - name: Ensure npm CLI supports trusted publishing
-        run: npm install -g npm@latest
+        run: |
+          echo "Node: $(node --version); bundled npm: $(npm --version)"
+          # Side-prefix install — avoids the arborist rebuild race that bites
+          # `npm install -g npm@latest` on Node 22.22.2's bundled npm 10.9.7
+          # (old arborist cannot find its own promise-retry after the global
+          # tree has been partially overwritten by the new npm mid-install).
+          INSTALL_DIR="$RUNNER_TEMP/npm-tp"
+          mkdir -p "$INSTALL_DIR"
+          npm install --prefix "$INSTALL_DIR" --no-save npm@latest
+          echo "$INSTALL_DIR/node_modules/.bin" >> "$GITHUB_PATH"
+          echo "Upgraded npm: $("$INSTALL_DIR/node_modules/.bin/npm" --version)"
 
       - name: Install uv
         uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v6
@@ -98,7 +113,14 @@ jobs:
       - name: Pre-publish dry-run (catch package validation failures before tag push)
         env:
           NPM_TOKEN: ""
-        run: npm publish --dry-run
+        run: |
+          VERSION="${{ steps.version.outputs.new_version }}"
+          if   [[ "$VERSION" == *"alpha"* ]]; then TAG=alpha
+          elif [[ "$VERSION" == *"beta"*  ]]; then TAG=beta
+          elif [[ "$VERSION" == *"rc"*    ]]; then TAG=rc
+          else                                     TAG=latest
+          fi
+          npm publish --dry-run --tag "$TAG"
 
       - name: Commit version bump
         run: |

<task-notification>
<task-id>a339dcbdf8c6cc0d4</task-id>
<tool-use-id>toolu_01TDqLgWkZMLhEoeoymQzpZT</tool-use-id>
<output-file>/tmp/claude-1000/-home-armel-Projects-OSS-bmad-module-skill-forge/1acd7c66-2a92-497b-a8f3-b7cde6fe6c75/tasks/a339dcbdf8c6cc0d4.output</output-file>
<status>completed</status>
<summary>Agent "Blind Hunter — diff-only review" completed</summary>
<result>- **PATH precedence may not actually use upgraded npm**
- **Location**: Lines `echo "$INSTALL_DIR/node_modules/.bin" >> "$GITHUB_PATH"` in the "Ensure npm CLI supports trusted publishing" step
- **Issue**: Appending to `$GITHUB_PATH` adds the directory to the END of PATH in subsequent steps, not the front. The `actions/setup-node@v4` step earlier likely prepends its own Node/npm bin directory to PATH, which contains the bundled npm 10.9.7. Subsequent steps calling `npm publish` will resolve to the bundled npm, not the upgraded one. The echo at the end of the same step confirms the upgrade works via absolute path, but that does nothing for later steps that just call `npm`.
- **Evidence**: `echo "$INSTALL_DIR/node_modules/.bin" >> "$GITHUB_PATH"` — GitHub's documented behavior is that `$GITHUB_PATH` prepends, but only for subsequent steps; however if setup-node's path is added the same way, ordering between them determines precedence. More importantly, the verification `echo "Upgraded npm: $("$INSTALL_DIR/node_modules/.bin/npm" --version)"` only proves the absolute path works, not that bare `npm` in later steps resolves to it.

- **Tag classification is substring-based and misclassifies versions**
- **Location**: Lines with `if [[ "$VERSION" == *"alpha"* ]]; then TAG=alpha` etc.
- **Issue**: Uses naive substring matching rather than semver prerelease parsing. A version like `1.0.0-rc.1+alpha-build` or any version containing these substrings anywhere (e.g., a metadata tag, a branch-name-influenced version, or a hypothetical `1.2.0-betazoid`) would be misclassified. Also, order matters: `1.0.0-alpha.rc1` would be tagged `alpha` even though it contains `rc`. More critically, `*"beta"*` would match `1.0.0-zeta-beta-testing` and `*"rc"*` would match anything containing the letters "rc" including `1.0.0-search`, `1.0.0-arch`, etc.
- **Evidence**: `if   [[ "$VERSION" == *"alpha"* ]]; then TAG=alpha` — no anchoring to the prerelease identifier section of semver.

- **Dry-run tag may diverge from actual publish tag**
- **Location**: Lines 113-123 (the modified "Pre-publish dry-run" step)

```diff
diff --git a/.github/workflows/release.yaml b/.github/workflows/release.yaml
index 4f5e6b1..9c67b9f 100644
--- a/.github/workflows/release.yaml
+++ b/.github/workflows/release.yaml
@@ -34,6 +34,11 @@ jobs:
   release:
     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
     environment: release
+    env:
+      # Husky's documented opt-out (.husky/_/h checks this explicitly). Needed
+      # because .husky/commit-msg invokes `entire` (local dev session tool) and
+      # hard-exits if it's not on PATH — breaks CI's `Commit version bump`.
+      HUSKY: "0"
     steps:
       - name: Checkout
         uses: actions/checkout@v4
@@ -49,7 +54,17 @@ jobs:
           registry-url: https://registry.npmjs.org
 
       - name: Ensure npm CLI supports trusted publishing
-        run: npm install -g npm@latest
+        run: |
+          echo "Node: $(node --version); bundled npm: $(npm --version)"
+          # Side-prefix install — avoids the arborist rebuild race that bites
+          # `npm install -g npm@latest` on Node 22.22.2's bundled npm 10.9.7
+          # (old arborist cannot find its own promise-retry after the global
+          # tree has been partially overwritten by the new npm mid-install).

+          npm install --prefix "$INSTALL_DIR" --no-save npm@latest
+          echo "$INSTALL_DIR/node_modules/.bin" >> "$GITHUB_PATH"
+          echo "Upgraded npm: $("$INSTALL_DIR/node_modules/.bin/npm" --version)"
 
       - name: Install uv
         uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v6
@@ -98,7 +113,14 @@ jobs:
       - name: Pre-publish dry-run (catch package validation failures before tag push)
         env:
           NPM_TOKEN: ""
-        run: npm publish --dry-run
+        run: |
+          VERSION="${{ steps.version.outputs.new_version }}"
+          if   [[ "$VERSION" == *"alpha"* ]]; then TAG=alpha
+          elif [[ "$VERSION" == *"beta"*  ]]; then TAG=beta
+          elif [[ "$VERSION" == *"rc"*    ]]; then TAG=rc
+          else                                     TAG=latest
+          fi
+          npm publish --dry-run --tag "$TAG"
 
       - name: Commit version bump
         run: |
```

You are the **Edge Case Hunter** for a code review. Your method: walk every branching path and boundary condition in the diff. Report only edge cases that are NOT handled. Orthogonal to adversarial critique — be exhaustive and method-driven.

You have full project read access. Read related files in the repo as needed to understand how the changed code plugs into the rest of the release workflow. DO NOT modify any files.

**Target file:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/.github/workflows/release.yaml`
**Diff file (scoped to this review):** `/tmp/story-3-2-review.diff`
**Repo root:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/`

The diff contains 5 commits of Story 3.2 defect-fixes applied after running an alpha release cut of the workflow. Four substantive changes:

1. **Job-level `HUSKY: "0"` env** — skips git hooks in CI (added because `.husky/commit-msg` invokes a local tool `entire` which is not on CI runners).
2. **Side-prefix npm self-upgrade** — replaces `npm install -g npm@latest` with an install into `$RUNNER_TEMP/npm-tp` and `$GITHUB_PATH` prepend, to avoid an arborist rebuild race.
3. **`--tag $TAG` in pre-publish dry-run** — derives dist-tag from version string (alpha/beta/rc/latest) so the dry-run matches the real publish step's dist-tag routing.
4. **Net-zero temp push trigger** — a throwaway `on: push` trigger was added and reverted in the same scope (not in the final diff).

For each changed block, enumerate edge cases:
- **`HUSKY: "0"` at job level:** does it cover every step that might invoke a git hook? Are there steps that `cd` into a sub-path, or explicitly call `git commit`? Does this break local dev expectations if `.husky/` configuration changes?
- **Side-prefix npm install:** what happens if `$RUNNER_TEMP` is unset? What if `npm@latest` pulls a version whose binary location differs (e.g. symlinks)? What if the install succeeds but `GITHUB_PATH` precedence is wrong and the bundled npm still wins? What if a later step calls `sudo npm` or a globally-installed tool that shelled out? Does the order of `$INSTALL_DIR/node_modules/.bin` in PATH actually take precedence over the Node-bundled npm?
- **Dist-tag derivation in dry-run:** what if version contains both `alpha` and `beta` (e.g. `1.0.0-alpha.beta.1`)? What about unusual prerelease strings like `rc.alpha` or `dev`? What if `$VERSION` is empty because `steps.version.outputs.new_version` was never set? Does this match how the REAL publish step downstream derives its tag?
- **Commented rationale vs reality:** Do the comments accurately describe what the code does? (The comments reference specific Node/npm versions — check whether those versions actually appear in repo config like `.nvmrc` / `package.json` engines.)

Also check the REAL publish step (downstream of the dry-run) in `release.yaml` — does the dry-run's `--tag` derivation logic match what the real publish step uses? Divergence between dry-run and real publish defeats the purpose of the dry-run gate.

Output findings as a Markdown bullet list. Each finding:
- **Title** (one line, starts with "Unhandled:" or "Mismatch:" or similar tag)
- **Trigger** (what input / state / branch condition surfaces it)
- **Current behavior** (what happens today — quote the line)
- **Gap** (what's not handled and why it matters)

If a path is handled, do NOT report it. Only report unhandled ones. Return ONLY the findings list — no preamble, no wrap-up.

- **Location**: Lines `echo "$INSTALL_DIR/node_modules/.bin" >> "$GITHUB_PATH"` in the "Ensure npm CLI supports trusted publishing" step
- **Issue**: Appending to `$GITHUB_PATH` adds the directory to the END of PATH in subsequent steps, not the front. The `actions/setup-node@v4` step earlier likely prepends its own Node/npm bin directory to PATH, which contains the bundled npm 10.9.7. Subsequent steps calling `npm publish` will resolve to the bundled npm, not the upgraded one. The echo at the end of the same step confirms the upgrade works via absolute path, but that does nothing for later steps that just call `npm`.
- **Evidence**: `echo "$INSTALL_DIR/node_modules/.bin" >> "$GITHUB_PATH"` — GitHub's documented behavior is that `$GITHUB_PATH` prepends, but only for subsequent steps; however if setup-node's path is added the same way, ordering between them determines precedence. More importantly, the verification `echo "Upgraded npm: $("$INSTALL_DIR/node_modules/.bin/npm" --version)"` only proves the absolute path works, not that bare `npm` in later steps resolves to it.

- **Tag classification is substring-based and misclassifies versions**
- **Location**: Lines with `if [[ "$VERSION" == *"alpha"* ]]; then TAG=alpha` etc.
- **Issue**: Uses naive substring matching rather than semver prerelease parsing. A version like `1.0.0-rc.1+alpha-build` or any version containing these substrings anywhere (e.g., a metadata tag, a branch-name-influenced version, or a hypothetical `1.2.0-betazoid`) would be misclassified. Also, order matters: `1.0.0-alpha.rc1` would be tagged `alpha` even though it contains `rc`. More critically, `*"beta"*` would match `1.0.0-zeta-beta-testing` and `*"rc"*` would match anything containing the letters "rc" including `1.0.0-search`, `1.0.0-arch`, etc.
- **Evidence**: `if   [[ "$VERSION" == *"alpha"* ]]; then TAG=alpha` — no anchoring to the prerelease identifier section of semver.

- **Dry-run tag may diverge from actual publish tag**
- **Location**: Lines 113-123 (the modified "Pre-publish dry-run" step)
- **Issue**: The dry-run now computes `TAG` locally, but if the real publish step (not shown in the diff) uses a different mechanism to determine the tag (e.g., `npm publish` default, or a separate action, or different logic), the dry-run could pass while the real publish fails, or vice versa. The comment says "match real publish step" but there's no guarantee the real step's logic was updated identically — we can't see it in the diff.
- **Evidence**: `npm publish --dry-run --tag "$TAG"` with inline bash logic — risks drift from the actual publish command.

- **`HUSKY=0` masks a real problem rather than fixing it**
- **Location**: Lines adding `env: HUSKY: "0"` at the job level
- **Issue**: The comment admits `.husky/commit-msg` invokes a local-only tool (`entire`) and hard-exits in CI. Disabling husky globally in CI means commit-msg validation (conventional commits, signoff, etc.) is bypassed for the `Commit version bump` step. If the repo later adds meaningful commit-msg checks, CI will silently skip them. The correct fix is making `.husky/commit-msg` CI-aware or guarding the `entire` invocation.
- **Evidence**: `HUSKY: "0"` with comment `hard-exits if it's not on PATH — breaks CI's Commit version bump`.

- **`--no-save` with `--prefix` to an empty dir still creates package.json side-effects**
- **Location**: Lines `npm install --prefix "$INSTALL_DIR" --no-save npm@latest`
- **Issue**: Installing into a fresh `$RUNNER_TEMP/npm-tp` directory with no pre-existing `package.json` means `--no-save` is meaningless (there's nothing to save to). More importantly, installing npm as a local dependency rather than globally can have quirks: the installed npm's internal paths may expect to be in a global location. Also, the `.bin` shim will be `npm -> ../npm/bin/npm-cli.js`, which should work, but npm's self-references (e.g., resolving its own `node_modules`) may behave unexpectedly when invoked outside a global context.
- **Evidence**: `npm install --prefix "$INSTALL_DIR" --no-save npm@latest` into `mkdir -p "$INSTALL_DIR"` (empty dir).

- **No verification that the upgraded npm version actually supports trusted publishing**
- **Location**: Lines in "Ensure npm CLI supports trusted publishing" step
- **Issue**: The step echoes the upgraded npm version but never asserts it meets the minimum version required for OIDC trusted publishing (npm 11.5.1+ per npm's docs). If `npm@latest` regresses or a registry issue serves an older version, the publish step later fails mysteriously. The previous `npm install -g npm@latest` had the same issue, but the step name promises an "Ensure" guarantee it doesn't deliver.
- **Evidence**: Step name `Ensure npm CLI supports trusted publishing` followed only by install + echo, no version gate.</result>

Not strictly a bug in the diff, but the `--tag` derivation change entrenches the illusion that the two steps are equivalent — they are not.

- **Mismatch: Comment cites specific versions that are NOT pinned in repo config**
  - **Trigger:** Any future Node upgrade, or CI runner image change that bumps the default Node 22.x minor version.
  - **Current behavior:** Lines 60-61 comment says the race bites "on Node 22.22.2's bundled npm 10.9.7". The repo's `.nvmrc` contains only `22` (major only), and `package.json` engines says `"node": ">=22.0.0"`. There is no `package-lock.json`-level pin for the Node/npm combo the comment asserts.
  - **Gap:** Rationale comment will become stale the moment `setup-node` resolves a newer 22.x minor. Maintainers reading the comment six months from now will not be able to reproduce the race to verify the workaround is still needed. No pin, no link to an upstream npm/arborist issue, no reproducer — the workaround will outlive its justification.

- **Unhandled: `Commit version bump` (line 125) uses `git commit -m "release: …"` — the `release:` prefix is not a conventional-commits type, and `commit-msg` hook would reject it if `HUSKY=0` were ever accidentally removed**
  - **Trigger:** A change that removes or mis-propagates the job-level `HUSKY: "0"` env (e.g. someone converts the job to a matrix and forgets to add env to the matrix).
  - **Current behavior:** `.husky/commit-msg` runs `entire hooks git commit-msg "$1" || exit 1`. `entire` likely validates conventional-commits format. `release:` is not a standard conventional-commits type (standard types are feat/fix/chore/docs/style/refactor/perf/test/build/ci/revert).
  - **Gap:** If `HUSKY=0` is ever dropped, the commit would fail both on (a) `entire` binary missing AND (b) non-conforming prefix. The workflow has only ONE safety net (HUSKY=0). No `--no-verify` defense, no conventional-commits-conformant prefix.

- **Unhandled: `Create and push tag` pushes the tag BEFORE `Publish to npm` — if the publish fails, the tag is already on the remote**
  - **Trigger:** Any failure in `Publish to npm via OIDC` (line 181) after `Create and push tag` (line 161) succeeded.
  - **Current behavior:** Line 167: `git push origin "v${...}"` executes unconditionally. Line 181 runs AFTER. If OIDC publish fails, the tag persists, `v$VERSION` exists on GitHub but no npm artifact — users `npm install …@$VERSION` see 404, users checking the Git tag see release.
  - **Gap:** The `--tag $TAG` dry-run is meant to "catch package validation failures before tag push" (per step name) — but does NOT catch OIDC failures. So the gate's claim of protecting tag-push from publish failures is incomplete; OIDC auth errors can still orphan a tag. Not introduced by this diff, but the diff's stated purpose ("match the real publish step's dist-tag routing") advertises a completeness the workflow doesn't actually provide.

- **Unhandled: `Push commit to main` (line 170) is gated on `github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'`, but `git push origin "v..."` (line 167) is NOT — a prerelease cut from a feature branch pushes the tag WITHOUT pushing the version-bump commit to main, orphaning the tag on a commit that doesn't live on main**
  - **Trigger:** `workflow_dispatch` from a feature branch (the "Story 3.2 alpha-cut pattern" explicitly documented on line 179).
  - **Current behavior:** Tag `v$VERSION` points to a commit that exists only on the feature branch. When the feature branch is deleted post-merge, the commit may be GC'd; tag becomes dangling unless GitHub retains it.
  - **Gap:** Not introduced by this diff, but `HUSKY=0` enables the `Commit version bump` step to succeed on feature branches, which is the enabling condition. If the tag commit is orphaned by feature-branch deletion, the `git describe --tags --abbrev=0` in subsequent releases (line 96) may fail or pick the wrong previous tag.

# Step 3: Triage

## RULES

- YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
- Be precise. When uncertain between categories, prefer the more conservative classification.

## INSTRUCTIONS

1. **Normalize** findings into a common format. Expected input formats:
   - Adversarial (Blind Hunter): markdown list of descriptions
   - Edge Case Hunter: JSON array with `location`, `trigger_condition`, `guard_snippet`, `potential_consequence` fields
   - Acceptance Auditor: markdown list with title, AC/constraint reference, and evidence

   If a layer's output does not match its expected format, attempt best-effort parsing. Note any parsing issues for the user.

   Convert all to a unified list where each finding has:
   - `id` -- sequential integer
   - `source` -- `blind`, `edge`, `auditor`, or merged sources (e.g., `blind+edge`)
   - `title` -- one-line summary
   - `detail` -- full description
   - `location` -- file and line reference (if available)

2. **Deduplicate.** If two or more findings describe the same issue, merge them into one:
   - Use the most specific finding as the base (prefer edge-case JSON with location over adversarial prose).
   - Append any unique detail, reasoning, or location references from the other finding(s) into the surviving `detail` field.
   - Set `source` to the merged sources (e.g., `blind+edge`).

3. **Classify** each finding into exactly one bucket:
   - **decision_needed** -- There is an ambiguous choice that requires human input. The code cannot be correctly patched without knowing the user's intent. Only possible if `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
   - **patch** -- Code issue that is fixable without human input. The correct fix is unambiguous.
   - **defer** -- Pre-existing issue not caused by the current change. Real but not actionable now.
   - **dismiss** -- Noise, false positive, or handled elsewhere.

   If `{review_mode}` = `"no-spec"` and a finding would otherwise be `decision_needed`, reclassify it as `patch` (if the fix is unambiguous) or `defer` (if not).

4. **Drop** all `dismiss` findings. Record the dismiss count for the summary.

5. If `{failed_layers}` is non-empty, report which layers failed before announcing results. If zero findings remain after dropping dismissed AND `{failed_layers}` is non-empty, warn the user that the review may be incomplete rather than announcing a clean review.

6. If zero findings remain after triage (all rejected or none raised): state "✅ Clean review — all layers passed." (Step 3 already warned if any review layers failed via `{failed_layers}`.)


## NEXT

Read fully and follow `./step-04-present.md`

Load the FULL `{sprint_status}` file. 104 2. Find the `development_status` entry matching `{story_key}`. 105 3. If found: update `development_status[{story_key}]` to `{new_status}`. Update `last_updated` to current date. Save the file, preserving ALL comments and structure including STATUS DEFINITIONS. 106 4. If `{story_key}` not found in sprint status: warn the user that the story file was updated but sprint-status sync failed. 107 108 If `{sprint_status}` file does not exist, note that story status was updated in the story file only. 109 110 #### Completion summary 111 112 > **Review Complete!** 113 > 114 > **Story Status:** `{new_status}` 115 > **Issues Fixed:** <fixed_count> 116 > **Action Items Created:** <action_count> 117 > **Deferred:** <W> 118 > **Dismissed:** <R> 119 120 ### 7. Next steps 121 122 Present the user with follow-up options: 123 124 > **What would you like to do next?** 125 > 1. **Start the next story** — run `dev-story` to pick up the next `ready-for-dev` story 126 > 2. **Re-run code review** — address findings and review again 127 > 3. **Done** — end the workflow 128 129 **HALT** — I am waiting for your choice. Do not proceed until the user selects an option. 130

718	| 2026-04-21 | 0.10.1-alpha.0 | First successful OIDC trusted publish of `bmad-module-skill-forge`; `release.yaml` validated end-to-end from `feat/release-workflow`; provenance attestation verified; 4 load-bearing defect-fixes applied to workflow during execution; allow-list widen/revoke cycle complete. | armel (dev agent claude-opus-4-7[1m]) |
719	
720	### Review Findings
721	
722	**Dev-agent-surfaced findings (pre-empting formal code review):**
723	
724	1. **[HIGH] CHANGELOG `[Unreleased]` anchor lifecycle is broken by `conventional-changelog-cli -s` append mode.** The preset prepends the new release entry at byte 0 of the infile, pushing `# Changelog` title (line 118), preamble (line 120), and `## [Unreleased]` anchor (line 122) BELOW the new release entry. Every subsequent release compounds the disorder. AC #10 answers Q1/Q2/Q3 documented in Debug Log References above. **Decision (user-approved 2026-04-21 at Story 3.2 close-out):** defer the fix to Story 3.3's merge PR rather than expand Story 3.2 scope. Recorded in `deferred-work.md § "Deferred from: implementation of 3-2-..."`. Story 3.3 adds a preamble-restore step to `release.yaml` (Python helper, sed/awk pipeline, or swapping generation approach to honor the `[Unreleased]` anchor as the insertion point). Story 5.1 is the backstop if Story 3.3 punts.
725	
726	2. **[MED] Dev agent wrote `AC #11` / `AC #12` in commit bodies; preset interpreted `#N` as GitHub issue refs.** The resulting CHANGELOG contains `closes [#11]` / `closes [#12]` links pointing at whatever issues bear those numbers in the repo (likely unrelated). The `Assert no bogus issue refs` guard correctly did NOT fire because these refs are numeric — the guard targets non-numeric refs (the Story 2.2 class-of-bug). Recommended process fix for all future Story dev agents: **write `AC N`, `AC-N`, or `acceptance criterion N` in commit bodies** — never `AC #N`. Story 5.1 audit can hand-correct the `0.10.1-alpha.0` CHANGELOG entry, or leave as historical artifact since the alpha is explicitly throwaway (AC #9 preserves it only as evidence).
727	
728	3. **[LOW] `f449c67` (first failed fix attempt) is dead code in the commit chain.** The `--force` approach was superseded by `cbc7851` (side-prefix install). Preserving per AC #11 "no amend, no force-push" rule. Story 3.3 merge PR will carry it as noise in the commit-list diff view. Acceptable cost for audit-trail integrity; no action needed beyond reviewer awareness.
729	
730	4. **[LOW] GitHub-hosted runner's bundled npm (Node 22.22.2 → npm 10.9.7) falls below the trusted-publisher floor of 11.5.1.** Documented in `cbc7851` commit body. The side-prefix install works but adds ~15s to CI time per run. Future optimization: re-check whether newer Node 22.x releases ship with npm ≥11.5.1 by default, then conditionally skip the bootstrap step. Not blocking; documented for Story 5.1's NFR1 cycle-time analysis.
731	
732	5. **[INFO] Node.js 20 deprecation warning on every run.** Actions `actions/checkout@v4`, `actions/setup-node@v4`, `astral-sh/setup-uv@v6`, and `softprops/action-gh-release@v2` are flagged as "running on Node.js 20" by the runner. GitHub will force Node 24 by 2026-06-02; Node 20 removed 2026-09-16. Two options: (a) rely on action maintainers to publish Node-24-compatible versions before the deadline (most have), (b) set `FORCE_JAVASCRIPT_ACTIONS_TO_NODE24=true` at workflow level. **Not a Story 3.2 concern** (the warnings fired on `feat/release-workflow` but also on `main` — this is a repo-wide upgrade scope). Flag for a future post-v1.0.0 maintenance story.
733	
734	6. **[INFO] `uv` setup action emits cache-miss warning every run.** "No file matched to ... requirements.txt ... pyproject.toml ... uv.lock". The repo has Python files (test:python subcommand uses pytest via uv) but no lockfile, so the uv cache never fills. Release.yaml still runs `npm test` successfully (Python tests included). Low priority; maintenance cleanup for a future story.
735	
736	**Deferred to formal review:**
737	
738	- Blind Hunter lens on OIDC surface (attestation payload shape at `.dist.attestations`, TP binding drift risk)
739	- Edge Case Hunter on version-string substring collisions (e.g., a future `1.0.0-march.1` would match `*"rc"*` in the case-chain — Story 3.1 code-review patch D didn't flag this; worth a second look)
740	- Acceptance Auditor line-by-line AC #1–#14 against Debug Log References evidence
741

| 2026-04-21 | 0.10.1-alpha.0 | First successful OIDC trusted publish of `bmad-module-skill-forge`; `release.yaml` validated end-to-end from `feat/release-workflow`; provenance attestation verified; 4 load-bearing defect-fixes applied to workflow during execution; allow-list widen/revoke cycle complete. | armel (dev agent claude-opus-4-7[1m]) |

### Review Findings

**Dev-agent-surfaced findings (pre-empting formal code review):**

1. **[HIGH] CHANGELOG `[Unreleased]` anchor lifecycle is broken by `conventional-changelog-cli -s` append mode.** The preset prepends the new release entry at byte 0 of the infile, pushing `# Changelog` title (line 118), preamble (line 120), and `## [Unreleased]` anchor (line 122) BELOW the new release entry. Every subsequent release compounds the disorder. AC #10 answers Q1/Q2/Q3 documented in Debug Log References above. **Decision (user-approved 2026-04-21 at Story 3.2 close-out):** defer the fix to Story 3.3's merge PR rather than expand Story 3.2 scope. Recorded in `deferred-work.md § "Deferred from: implementation of 3-2-..."`. Story 3.3 adds a preamble-restore step to `release.yaml` (Python helper, sed/awk pipeline, or swapping generation approach to honor the `[Unreleased]` anchor as the insertion point). Story 5.1 is the backstop if Story 3.3 punts.

2. **[MED] Dev agent wrote `AC #11` / `AC #12` in commit bodies; preset interpreted `#N` as GitHub issue refs.** The resulting CHANGELOG contains `closes [#11]` / `closes [#12]` links pointing at whatever issues bear those numbers in the repo (likely unrelated). The `Assert no bogus issue refs` guard correctly did NOT fire because these refs are numeric — the guard targets non-numeric refs (the Story 2.2 class-of-bug). Recommended process fix for all future Story dev agents: **write `AC N`, `AC-N`, or `acceptance criterion N` in commit bodies** — never `AC #N`. Story 5.1 audit can hand-correct the `0.10.1-alpha.0` CHANGELOG entry, or leave as historical artifact since the alpha is explicitly throwaway (AC #9 preserves it only as evidence).

3. **[LOW] `f449c67` (first failed fix attempt) is dead code in the commit chain.** The `--force` approach was superseded by `cbc7851` (side-prefix install). Preserving per AC #11 "no amend, no force-push" rule. Story 3.3 merge PR will carry it as noise in the commit-list diff view. Acceptable cost for audit-trail integrity; no action needed beyond reviewer awareness.

4. **[LOW] GitHub-hosted runner's bundled npm (Node 22.22.2 → npm 10.9.7) falls below the trusted-publisher floor of 11.5.1.** Documented in `cbc7851` commit body. The side-prefix install works but adds ~15s to CI time per run. Future optimization: re-check whether newer Node 22.x releases ship with npm ≥11.5.1 by default, then conditionally skip the bootstrap step. Not blocking; documented for Story 5.1's NFR1 cycle-time analysis.

5. **[INFO] Node.js 20 deprecation warning on every run.** Actions `actions/checkout@v4`, `actions/setup-node@v4`, `astral-sh/setup-uv@v6`, and `softprops/action-gh-release@v2` are flagged as "running on Node.js 20" by the runner. GitHub will force Node 24 by 2026-06-02; Node 20 removed 2026-09-16. Two options: (a) rely on action maintainers to publish Node-24-compatible versions before the deadline (most have), (b) set `FORCE_JAVASCRIPT_ACTIONS_TO_NODE24=true` at workflow level. **Not a Story 3.2 concern** (the warnings fired on `feat/release-workflow` but also on `main` — this is a repo-wide upgrade scope). Flag for a future post-v1.0.0 maintenance story.

6. **[INFO] `uv` setup action emits cache-miss warning every run.** "No file matched to ... requirements.txt ... pyproject.toml ... uv.lock". The repo has Python files (test:python subcommand uses pytest via uv) but no lockfile, so the uv cache never fills. Release.yaml still runs `npm test` successfully (Python tests included). Low priority; maintenance cleanup for a future story.

**Deferred to formal review:**

- Blind Hunter lens on OIDC surface (attestation payload shape at `.dist.attestations`, TP binding drift risk)
- Edge Case Hunter on version-string substring collisions (e.g., a future `1.0.0-march.1` would match `*"rc"*` in the case-chain — Story 3.1 code-review patch D didn't flag this; worth a second look)
- Acceptance Auditor line-by-line AC #1–#14 against Debug Log References evidence
